name: La Sagra – circular from Valmojado through Casarrubios and Chozas
name_original: La Sagra – circular desde Valmojado por Casarrubios y Chozas
region_intro: Road cycling around Valmojado, located in the province of Toledo, offers diverse landscapes characterized by the "Toledo steppe." The region features extensive cereal fields, olive groves, and vineyards, providing quiet, undulating roads suitable for road cyclists. Mediterranean scrubland, holm oaks, and gall oaks are also present in areas like the Dehesa de Valdeniebla. The terrain generally consists of smooth surfaces with varying elevation profiles. Best road cycling routes around Valmojado The most popular road cycling route is Between Toledo and Madrid – circular from Valmojado to Navalcarnero, a 48.9 miles (78.6 km) trail that takes 3 hours 19 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects Valmojado with Navalcarnero, traversing the characteristic landscapes of the region. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Sagra – circular from Valmojado through Casarrubios and Chozas, an easy 26.1 miles (42.0 km) path. This route explores the Sagra region, passing through towns like Casarrubios del Monte. Local road cyclists also love the Casillas – Casillas loop from Méntrida, a 71.6 miles (115.2 km) trail leading through varied terrain with significant elevation gain, often completed in about 5 hours 31 minutes. Road cycling around Valmojado is defined by extensive cereal fields, olive groves, and undulating roads. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging long-distance routes. The routes in Valmojado are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 700 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valmojado's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling in Valmojado?
A: The region around Valmojado is characterized by its "Toledo steppe" environment, featuring extensive cereal fields, olive groves, and vineyards. You'll find quiet, undulating roads with generally smooth surfaces. Areas like the Dehesa de Valdeniebla offer picturesque Mediterranean scrubland with holm oaks and gall oaks, providing varied scenery.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for different skill levels in Valmojado?
A: Yes, Valmojado offers a range of road cycling routes for various abilities. Out of 14 available routes, you'll find 2 easy routes, 7 moderate routes, and 5 more challenging options with significant elevation gain, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What are some popular circular road cycling routes around Valmojado?
A: Many cyclists enjoy circular routes in the area. A popular moderate option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027132659/between-toledo-and-madrid-circular-from-valmojado-to-navalcarnero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Between Toledo and Madrid – circular from Valmojado to Navalcarnero&lt;/a&gt;, which covers nearly 79 km. For an easier ride, consider &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027131573/la-sagra-circular-from-valmojado-through-casarrubios-and-chozas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sagra – circular from Valmojado through Casarrubios and Chozas&lt;/a&gt;, a 42 km path exploring the Sagra region.

Q: What interesting sights can I see along the road cycling routes in Valmojado?
A: Beyond the scenic landscapes of cereal fields and olive groves, you can encounter several points of interest. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027131573/la-sagra-circular-from-valmojado-through-casarrubios-and-chozas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sagra – circular from Valmojado through Casarrubios and Chozas&lt;/a&gt; passes through &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3929073" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casarrubios del Monte Historical Center&lt;/a&gt;, known for its 14th-century Mudejar-style castle. Another route, &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027132659/between-toledo-and-madrid-circular-from-valmojado-to-navalcarnero" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Between Toledo and Madrid – circular from Valmojado to Navalcarnero&lt;/a&gt;, can lead you near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2675439" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Navalcarnero Main Square&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical sites to explore on a ride around Valmojado?
A: Absolutely. The region is rich in history. You can cycle past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3929073" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casarrubios del Monte Historical Center&lt;/a&gt;, which features a notable 14th-century Mudejar-style castle. Valmojado itself boasts a recently restored 15th-century Church of Santo Domingo de Guzmán, a local windmill offering panoramic views, and five historic fountains, some dating back eight centuries.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Valmojado?
A: The best seasons for road cycling in Valmojado are typically spring (April to June) and autumn (September to October). During these months, the weather is generally mild and pleasant, making for comfortable riding conditions. Summers can be very hot, and winters can be chilly, though cycling is possible year-round.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Valmojado?
A: The road cycling routes in Valmojado are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Cyclists often praise the quiet, undulating roads that traverse extensive cereal fields, olive groves, and vineyards, offering a peaceful and scenic experience away from heavy traffic.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes near Valmojado?
A: While specific family-friendly routes are not detailed, the region's generally quiet, undulating roads through agricultural landscapes can be suitable for families. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1027131573/la-sagra-circular-from-valmojado-through-casarrubios-and-chozas" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Sagra – circular from Valmojado through Casarrubios and Chozas&lt;/a&gt; route is classified as easy and covers 42 km, which might be a good option for families looking for a less strenuous ride.

Q: Can I find cafes or restaurants that are cyclist-friendly in Valmojado?
A: Valmojado, as a local town, offers several cafes and restaurants where cyclists can stop for refreshments. While not explicitly branded as 'cyclist-friendly,' local establishments are generally welcoming. You'll find options in the town center, particularly around the Plaza Mayor, to refuel during or after your ride.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Valmojado?
A: Yes, parking is generally available in Valmojado, especially in and around the town center. As a smaller town, you should be able to find suitable spots to park your vehicle before starting your cycling adventure. Always check local signage for any restrictions.

Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Valmojado?
A: There are 14 distinct road cycling routes available around Valmojado, offering a variety of distances and difficulty levels for exploration.

Q: Are there any long-distance road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are longer routes with significant elevation gain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31884207" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Casillas – Casillas loop from Méntrida&lt;/a&gt;, for example, is a difficult 115 km trail with over 1500 meters of elevation gain, typically taking over 5 hours to complete.
tips: Valmojado, as its name already suggests, has a close relationship with water and the underground journeys it makes through the municipality. These trips are manifested in the Five Fountains (Quique Fontes) that are located at the main access points to the town. Each of these sources has its own name: Fuente del Caño Viejo or Fresco, Fuente del Indiano, Fuente de la Teja, Caño de Méntrida and Fuente de la Salud.

This town of medieval splendor stands out for its walls and its castle, curiously built outside the walls as its function was purely defensive. It was a place of great importance until the 16th century, on the same level as other cities such as Segovia. Today it is a good place to stop on a route through the area.

If you are interested in the phenomena of real estate speculation, you can take a ride with your bike through the successive extensions that were made in this agricultural town in the 2000s, when it was intended to turn it into a new "dormitory city" for Madrid. The town went from having 500 to 2,000 homes, many of which were never inhabited, or even completed. The town went through a tough time after the real estate crisis with high unemployment rates and the hangover of this recent history still lingers today.

waypoints.name: Walls and Castle of Casarrubios del Monte
